participants.reasoning: The extracted participant description matches the official record on sample size, setting, trauma types, age range, and key inclusion/exclusion criteria, with only minor differences in wording and the note that baseline characteristics were reported for the 29 completers.
participants.extracted: Thirty-one trauma-exposed adults aged 18 to 65 years with acute stress disorder (full DSM-IV ASD or intrusion and hyperarousal criteria) were enrolled within 4 weeks of a traumatic event such as a motor vehicle collision, accident, physical assault, or sexual assault. Participants were recruited from University of Cape Town–affiliated hospitals and clinics in South Africa. Two participants were not contactable before the first post-baseline assessment, leaving 29 participants in the intent-to-treat sample: 12 assigned to escitalopram and 17 to placebo. The sample included men and women, with a mean age of about 30 years, and most had experienced assault; other trauma types included motor vehicle accidents and witnessing traumatic events. Individuals with serious physical injury, major psychiatric comorbidity, high suicide risk, recent use of prohibited medications, pregnancy/breastfeeding, or significant medical illness were excluded.

interventions.reasoning: Both descriptions match on escitalopram vs matching placebo, starting at 10 mg daily with increase to 20 mg at week 4 if tolerated, allowing dose reduction for side effects, and continuing for a 24-week treatment period.
interventions.extracted: Eligible participants were randomized to receive escitalopram or matching placebo. The active treatment was escitalopram 10 mg capsules, identical in appearance to placebo and dispensed in sequentially numbered blister packs. Participants were instructed to take one 10 mg capsule daily starting at baseline (week 0/visit 2), with the dose increased to 20 mg per day at week 4 if tolerated. Dose reduction back to 10 mg daily was allowed for intolerable side effects. Treatment continued through week 24, with participants followed thereafter for maintenance of effect up to week 56.

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes align with the official record on the key measures and time points, with only additional secondary outcomes in the extraction that do not conflict with the official list.
outcomes.extracted: Primary outcome: prevention of PTSD symptoms, measured as change from baseline (week 0/visit 2) to week 24 on the Clinician Administered PTSD Scale (CAPS) total score; PTSD diagnosis was also assessed using the CAPS dichotomous cutoff of ≥50 points at planned follow-up assessments. Secondary outcomes: PTSD symptom maintenance/change from baseline to week 56 on the CAPS; global clinical severity and improvement using the Clinical Global Impression scales (CGI-S and CGI-I) at repeated assessments through week 24 and follow-up to week 56; depression severity using the Montgomery-Asberg Depression Rating Scale (MADRS) at repeated assessments through week 24 and follow-up to week 56; self-reported depression using the Visual Analogue Scale for Depression (VAS-D) at repeated assessments through week 24 and follow-up to week 56; self-reported anxiety using the Visual Analogue Scale for Anxiety (VAS-A) at repeated assessments through week 24 and follow-up to week 56; functional impairment/disability using the Sheehan Disability Scale (SDS) at repeated assessments through week 24 and follow-up to week 56; presence of any MINI psychiatric diagnosis using the MINI 5.0.0 at repeated assessments through week 24 and follow-up to week 56; and adverse events, including withdrawal due to adverse events, monitored throughout treatment.
